feedback:
Overall, the model shows emerging but inconsistent malware-analysis capability. Exact-match accuracy is modest, but the average Jaccard score indicates that the model usually identifies at least some of the correct options. The dominant failure mode is over-selection: it frequently adds plausible-sounding but incorrect options, which severely hurts exact-match performance even when core reasoning is partially correct.

The model performs best on infostealer-related network IOC questions and EDR/AV killer classification, and it is reasonably strong on ransomware behavior identification. It is noticeably weaker on more abstract MITRE ATT&CK mappings and on nuanced defense-evasion / unhooking questions, where it tends to conflate related techniques and select too many options.

Given the low parse error rate, formatting and extraction of option letters are not a problem; the main issues are judgment and calibration of how many answers to choose. The model often includes distractors that are adjacent in meaning to correct behaviors (e.g., other defense-evasion or persistence techniques that are present in the report but not asked for in the specific question).

For further training, focus on:
- Multi-label calibration: exercises where the number of correct options varies, with explicit feedback on when to stop adding plausible but unsupported options.
- Fine-grained ATT&CK mapping: distinguishing closely related techniques (e.g., different defense-evasion methods, different persistence types) based strictly on evidence in the detonation report.
- Defense evasion / unhooking and process injection: practice questions that require precise identification of injection type and evasion mechanism, penalizing over-selection.
- Medium-difficulty, multi-correct questions across all attack families, with emphasis on reading and prioritizing the exact question wording (e.g., “most strongly indicate”, “rather than”, or “specifically demonstrate ransomware-style inhibition”).

The model tends to over-select answers: on average it chooses more options than are actually correct, and most of its errors are due to adding extra distractors rather than missing correct items. It is comparatively better on easy items and on infostealer / Remcos RAT classification and network C2 detection, but weaker on medium-difficulty ATT&CK mapping and UM-unhooking defense evasion.

With targeted training on option calibration and fine-grained technique discrimination, the model could move from basic to solid competence on this benchmark.
strengths: Low parse error rate and consistent extraction of option letters.
Good performance on infostealer-related network IOC and C2 behavior questions, including multi-correct items.
Strong understanding of ransomware behavioral patterns such as shadow copy deletion, backup catalog removal, ransom notes, and double-extension file renaming.
Competent classification of EDR/AV killer behavior via API hooking and KsecDD interaction.
Reasonable performance on Remcos RAT family identification and some associated MITRE ATT&CK techniques.

weaknesses.description: The model frequently selects more options than are correct, adding plausible but unsupported distractors. This is the dominant cause of exact-match failures, even when the core correct options are included.

On UM-unhooking and broader defense-evasion questions, the model often confuses different evasion mechanisms and either omits required options or adds unrelated ones, showing difficulty in mapping specific behaviors to the correct techniques.
The model struggles with precise ATT&CK technique mapping, especially for Remcos and UM-unhooking scenarios, often missing one of several required techniques or adding extra ones.
Performance on medium-difficulty questions with multiple correct options is uneven; the model frequently makes near-miss errors by adding one extra distractor or omitting one required option.

weaknesses.area_name: AV/EDR killers and UM-unhooking fine-grained distinctions
weaknesses.description: While the model can recognize high-level EDR/AV killer behavior, it is less reliable at distinguishing between generic infostealer activity and security-component tampering, and at identifying unhooking-specific patterns.
